speaker: Troy
en: Hey Gabriella, how is it going?

speaker: Gabriella
en: I've been pretty busy lately.

speaker: Troy
en: Oh, what are you up to?

speaker: Gabriella
en: I've been working on a dissertation on music education specializing in music performance.

speaker: Troy
en: Sounds intriguing.

speaker: Troy
en: What have you found?

speaker: Gabriella
en: Well, I interviewed 18 freshmen majoring in music performance as my sample population.

speaker: Gabriella
en: I first asked about their music background before being accepted to music schools.

speaker: Troy
en: Let me guess, have they all got relevant qualifications in music?

speaker: Gabriella
en: Well, even though there is a wealth of music qualifications available, most of the students didn't receive any until they entered music schools.

speaker: Gabriella
en: Some capabilities like music production or music technology are pretty tough going.

speaker: Troy
en: Now that you've mentioned it, I recall that I got mine in sound engineering in my sophomore year.

speaker: Gabriella
en: Right.

speaker: Gabriella
en: But the majority of these students admitted that they had performed in front of a large crowd.

speaker: Gabriella
en: Around 80% of them had performed on stage as band members in secondary schools.

speaker: Gabriella
en: A few of them played piano or guitar at the time.

speaker: Gabriella
en: And it also surprised me that they were generally able to play multiple instruments with proficiency.

speaker: Troy
en: Have they tried to compose a piece of music before?

speaker: Gabriella
en: Only in rare cases did they have prior experience in composition.

speaker: Troy
en: They would have to do it sooner or later.

speaker: Troy
en: There is a whole module for it.

speaker: Troy
en: What did they say about practising?

speaker: Gabriella
en: All subjects mentioned that they feared being marked too low and tried their best.

speaker: Troy
en: So they were like practising every single day?

speaker: Gabriella
en: Not for most people.

speaker: Gabriella
en: Some students worked with others in a band, so they had to arrange the time for rehearsals.

speaker: Gabriella
en: But all 18 students had sufficient amount of practice.

speaker: Gabriella
en: And they frequently mentioned that playing technical pieces was exhilarating.

speaker: Troy
en: I feel that way, too.

speaker: Gabriella
en: And then I asked questions about how they chose the pieces that were assessed and their attempts to improve their performance in recital classes.

speaker: Troy
en: What did they say?

speaker: Gabriella
en: Two students mentioned that they always asked their tutors' advice on the pieces they were going to perform because they felt that would give them a competitive edge over those who had no clue of tutors' preference in music.

speaker: Troy
en: Well, that's a bit sneaky.

speaker: Gabriella
en: But the most common remark was that they had a minimal interest in pieces that the public barely knew.

speaker: Gabriella
en: Instead, most prefer popular pieces that other students would also choose.

speaker: Troy
en: How come?

speaker: Gabriella
en: They said that this could mirror their weaknesses and thus help them to perfect the techniques for handling the most difficult bits.

speaker: Gabriella
en: All but one of the 18 students admitted that to perform well, they had to learn the music by heart, or otherwise, they would have to divert their attention and thus make some errors.

speaker: Troy
en: I agree.

speaker: Gabriella
en: Then I asked them about their thoughts on practising in different aspects.

speaker: Gabriella
en: And some of their answers were entirely unexpected.

speaker: Troy
en: Oh, what aspects did you look into?

speaker: Gabriella
en: I first asked how they feel about playing solo.

speaker: Troy
en: What did they say?

speaker: Gabriella
en: They said being a solo performer takes desire, focus and hard work.

speaker: Gabriella
en: And the performance would not pull off if one took the stage without confidence.

speaker: Gabriella
en: But they all took great pleasure in it.

speaker: Troy
en: Yeah, that's quite different from playing in an orchestra, where musicians have to work with other members to produce a harmonious sound that is pleasing to the ear.

speaker: Gabriella
en: Right.

speaker: Gabriella
en: And I also asked whether the choice of the piece to play would affect their performance.

speaker: Troy
en: Well, that's a big yes for me.

speaker: Troy
en: When I play a song I don't like, I won't devote myself to it.

speaker: Gabriella
en: That's widely accepted.

speaker: Gabriella
en: The audience can tell when performers are being authentic and inspired by the music they are performing.

speaker: Gabriella
en: Many would spend hours or even days choosing the right piece.

speaker: Troy
en: You have to put a lot of thought into it before it's too late.

speaker: Troy
en: So what kind of musical genre do they prefer?

speaker: Gabriella
en: The answers vary vastly.

speaker: Gabriella
en: But one thing in common is that they rarely switch from one style to another.

speaker: Troy
en: Of course.

speaker: Troy
en: It is tough to make such a transition.

speaker: Troy
en: Most instrumentalists initially start with some classical based training.

speaker: Troy
en: But they don't know how to take their already existing knowledge and apply it to a new style of music.

speaker: Troy
en: What about their feedback on playing duets?

speaker: Gabriella
en: Yes, that's the last question on my list.

speaker: Gabriella
en: I asked their take on playing a duet with someone with the same musical instrument, like a violin duet.

speaker: Gabriella
en: Although it is pretty demanding to do so, they all admitted that it is helpful in that it renews their motivation while enhancing their technical skills.

speaker: Troy
en: So, have you started writing the 1st draft of your dissertation?

speaker: Troy
en: I can help if...
